title: How the osteoclast degrades bone
abstract:
How the osteoclast
degrades bone
Harry C. Blair
Summary
Osteoclasts are multinucleated monocyte-macrophage derivatives that degrade
bone. Their specialized role is central to a process that continuously removes and
replaces segments of the skeleton in the higher vertebrates. Osteoclasts allow
skeletal mineral to be used to manage extracellular calcium activity, which is an
important adaptation for life on land, and solid skeletal structure to be replaced by
hollow architecture that has a superior strength-to-weight ratio. Degrading bone
also allows periodic repair and remodeling for ordered growth and efficient
response to mechanical loads. A fairly comprehensive view of osteoclastic ontog-
eny and function is emerging from recent studies. Osteoclasts dissolve bone
mineral by massive acid secretion and secrete specialized proteinases that
degrade the organic matrix, mainly type I collagen, in this acidic milieu. The site
of bone dissolution is a high-calcium environment; removal of degradation prod-
ucts by transcytosis of membrane vesicles allows the osteoclast to maintain a
normal intracellular calcium. Osteoclastic differentiation is normally balanced with
bone formation, although bone formation is the function of unrelated stromal
cell-derived osteoblasts. Interactions between osteoclast precursors and bone-
forming cells are believed to control osteoclast differentiation under most circum-
stances, preserving bone architecture over many cycles of bone replacement.
BioEssays 20:837–846, 1998.
r 1998 John Wiley & Sons, Inc.
Derivation of the osteoclast
The endoskeleton developed to support muscles in ancestral
vertebrates that lived in stable salt-water environments. In
chordates without bone, such as chondrichthyes, this func-
tion is largely unchanged. However, mineralization of the
skeleton to produce primitive bone allowed additional devel-
opment. About 80% of bone matrix is hydroxyapatite,
[Ca3(PO4)2]3CaOH2, a potential source of calcium or base
equivalents. Because calcium is used widely in cell signaling,
an organism capable of degrading bone would have addi-
tional capacity for calcium homeostasis, an advantage in
low-calcium environments. Cells that degrade bone, osteo-
clasts, appear in teleost ﬁshes,(1) which move from salt water
to fresh water. Under low-calcium conditions, osteoclasts
form by fusion of circulating precursor cells on the bone
surface under the control of calcium-sensing branchial cells.
Calcium homeostasis is controlled by branchial cleft organs,
the parathyroid glands, even in the higher vertebrates. These
glands are composed of calcium-sensing cells that produce
parathyroid hormone,(2) a polypeptide.
The use of skeletal mineral in metabolic regulation is
particularly important in terrestrial vertebrates, where osteo-
clasts are highly specialized. Adaptations increase the effi-
ciency of bone degradation and add control mechanisms that
balance skeletal electrolyte management functions with main-
tenance of skeletal architecture. Skeletal weight is a disadvan-
tage in life on land, which led to the development of a
hollowed-out architecture with vastly improved strength per
weight. The osteoclast is intimately involved in the formation
of this advanced bone architecture. Osteoclasts remove
mineralized cartilage that is replaced with bone, which occurs
in embryogenesis and at cartilaginous growth plates during
Funding agency: National Institutes of Health; Grant numbers:
AG12951; AR43225.
Correspondence to: Harry Blair, 535 LHRB, 701 S. 19th Street,
Birmingham, AL 35294-0007. E-mail: blair@lh.path.uab.edu

BioEssays 20:837–846, r 1998 John Wiley & Sons, Inc.
BioEssays 20.10
837
 growth. Osteoclasts also remove damaged bone for replace-
ment.
The remainder of this essay will focus on the osteoclast in
the highly developed bone of the air-breathing vertebrates.
Primitive bone and embryogenesis of bone will not be
considered further.(3) Additional properties of bone in terres-
trial vertebrates that are important to osteoclast function are
described in the next section. Following this, osteoclast
cellular specializations and disorders caused by biochemical
defects are discussed. Mechanisms that control osteoclastic
differentiation and activity, which are incompletely understood
and controversial, are discussed at the end.
In terrestrial vertebrates, the skeleton is a living
organ that is replaced regularly
In the air-breathing vertebrates, mature bones consist of
dense surface plates of bone, the cortices, within which there
is a web of delicate bone struts oriented to oppose loading
forces, the trabecular bone. The bone-forming cells, osteo-
blasts, differentiate continuously from pleuripotential stromal
cells, which also produce ﬁbroblasts and adipocytes.(4) The
osteoblasts secrete an organic bone matrix, over 90% heavily
cross-linked type I collagen, which is oriented along lines of
stress, and then deposit the hydroxyapatite mineral in the
matrix, which resists compression. This design is light-weight
and strong, although the bone ﬂexes several percent under
load and the structure is subject to wear or fracture if its
elastic modulus is exceeded. Parathyroid hormone promotes
bone synthesis by osteoblasts as well as osteoclast formation
and activity, the combination of bone synthesis and degrada-
tion generally occurring together in a turnover process.
This complex architecture has a large surface area that is
ideal to support cycles of appositional synthesis and surface
degradation during load-bearing stress. The hollow marrow
space is occupied by adipose tissue and hematopoietic cells
in variable proportions. A section of avian trabecular bone is
shown in Figure 1A. The entire bone surface is cellular.
Multinucleated osteoclasts are typically found in groups
adjacent to each other. Osteoblasts are also found in cohort
groups with the same activity. This specimen was taken from
a hyperparathyroid animal to emphasize the active bone-
forming and -degrading processes; in a normal animal, much
of the skeleton is lined by ﬂat, synthetically inactive osteo-
blasts. Bone is impermeable and bone cells, particularly
osteoclasts, are delicate. Without special ﬁxation and section-
ing, the cellular structure is difficult to appreciate, and de-
nuded matrix surfaces in poorly ﬁxed bone have given rise to
numerous misconceptions regarding bone metabolism. The
physiological interface of bone with other cells or body ﬂuids
is via osteoclasts or osteoblasts; exceptions are the pathologi-
cal cases of fracture or necrosis.
Osteoblasts become embedded in the bone they produce
(arrows, Fig. 1A); a cohort of adjacent osteoblasts typically
produces an ,7-µm-thick lamina of bone in about a week.
The embedded cells remain alive and are called osteocytes;
their function is poorly understood. Osteoblasts also produce
other matrix proteins, including the widely expressed integrin-
binding arginyl-glycyl-aspartyl-containing osteopontin and the
g-carboxyglutamate-containing osteocalcin (which is speciﬁc
to bone). Specialized matrix proteins, interleukins, and growth
factors that are produced by osteoblasts affect bone structure
and bone–cell interactions in various ways.(5,6) A characteris-
tic posttranslationally modiﬁed alkaline pH-optimum phospha-
tase that is important to mineral deposition is secreted by
osteoblasts; mineral deposition is promoted by alkaline condi-
tions. The osteoblast also has a quiescent phase that does
not synthesize bone. Humans typically replace trabecular
bone in ,3–4 years, although the rate is variable and often
lower, particularly in old age. The dynamics of bone turnover
are demonstrated using tetracycline; this ﬂuorescent antibi-
otic is adsorbed to bone and labels bone synthesis. New bone
formed in the interval between two periods of tetracycline
administration in a human bone biopsy is illustrated in Figure
1B. Since formation and degradation are (normally) bal-
anced, osteoclasts degrade bone at approximately the same
rate. The dense and relatively inﬂexible bone cortex also
turns over, but normally less than once per decade after the
skeleton reaches adult size.
Macrophages are cells with particular adaptation for acid
degradation, and bone mineral is soluble in acid. Thus, it is
logical that osteoclasts are related to macrophages. Differen-
tiation of osteoclasts from circulating precursors(7) of the
monocyte–macrophage family(8,9) has been demonstrated
conclusively. This ontogeny is dissimilar to the primitive
mesenchymal precursors of bone-forming cells. Osteoclasts
are characterized by massive expression of a cell-surface
vacuolar-like H1-ATPase(10) and related proteins that produce
the acid which dissolves bone mineral and secrete a charac-
teristic tartrate-resistant acid phosphatase.(11) Osteoclast dif-
ferentiation involves cell fusion in all osteoclast-producing
vertebrate classes. Cell fusion increases the area in contact
with bone relative to the circumference of the cell–bone
attachment, which must be sealed to produce the acid
environment, an advantage in maintaining the pH gradient
used for bone dissolution. Osteoclasts contain from 3–5 to
12–15 nuclei depending on the species (Fig. 2). Within a
species, nuclei per cell may vary with the activity of bone
turnover. Mitochondria are abundant in osteoclasts, reﬂecting
the energy-intensive task of dissolving bone (Fig. 2B). Active
osteoclasts have at their interface with bone a highly folded
plasma membrane with a large surface area, the ruffled
membrane (Fig. 2C). This expanded membrane surface
supports the apparatus for massive HCl transport. In addition,
osteoclasts contain vacuoles of various kinds, which function
in secretory and transport processes.

Macrophages are cells with particular adaptation for acid
degradation, and bone mineral is soluble in acid. Thus, it is
logical that osteoclasts are related to macrophages. Differen-
tiation of osteoclasts from circulating precursors(7) of the
monocyte–macrophage family(8,9) has been demonstrated
conclusively. This ontogeny is dissimilar to the primitive
mesenchymal precursors of bone-forming cells. Osteoclasts
are characterized by massive expression of a cell-surface
vacuolar-like H1-ATPase(10) and related proteins that produce
the acid which dissolves bone mineral and secrete a charac-
teristic tartrate-resistant acid phosphatase.(11) Osteoclast dif-
ferentiation involves cell fusion in all osteoclast-producing
vertebrate classes. Cell fusion increases the area in contact
with bone relative to the circumference of the cell–bone
attachment, which must be sealed to produce the acid
environment, an advantage in maintaining the pH gradient
used for bone dissolution. Osteoclasts contain from 3–5 to
12–15 nuclei depending on the species (Fig. 2). Within a
species, nuclei per cell may vary with the activity of bone
turnover. Mitochondria are abundant in osteoclasts, reﬂecting
the energy-intensive task of dissolving bone (Fig. 2B). Active
osteoclasts have at their interface with bone a highly folded
plasma membrane with a large surface area, the ruffled
membrane (Fig. 2C). This expanded membrane surface
supports the apparatus for massive HCl transport. In addition,
osteoclasts contain vacuoles of various kinds, which function
in secretory and transport processes.
The mechanism of bone degradation
The mechanism of bone turnover has been investigated
intensively for decades, driven by the general interest of this
fundamental biological process as well as its practical impor-
tance in diseases such as osteoporosis, which afflict large
numbers of people. The ﬁrst attempts to understand the
cellular mechanisms of bone turnover were descriptive, and
this led to controversial interpretations. However, it is impor-
tant to acknowledge that signiﬁcant observations included
vital-dye studies, suggesting acidic conditions at the bone-
Figure 1. Bone cells and bone turnover.
A: Cell structure of avian trabecular bone.
An undecalciﬁed section, stained with meth-
ylene blue. The cells and nuclei are violet;
mineralized matrix is impermeable and ex-
cludes the dye (Bone). Nonmineralized
bone matrix is seen as a blue band of
varying thickness around each bone tra-
becula. Note that the entire bone surface is
cellular. Osteoclasts are multinucleated gi-
ant cells that degrade bone matrix (oc).
Groups of osteoclasts typically degrade
contiguous sections of bone. The square
delineates an area of osteoclasts attached
to bone similar to the higher-power view in
Figure 2A. Bone-forming osteoblasts (ob)
are cuboidal cells that synthesize bone.
Cohorts of contiguous osteoblasts are con-
nected by gap junctions and act as a unit.
Osteoblasts secrete a collagenous matrix
and then mineralize it; during bone forma-
tion, waves of osteoblasts differentiate from
mesenchymal precursors. Osteoblasts em-
bedded in the matrix they produce are
called osteocytes; a few osteocytes are
indicated by arrows. Osteocytes retain cel-
lular processes that communicate with ad-
jacent cells. The animal used was given a
low-calcium diet to increase bone turnover
and emphasize cellular activity. Most of the
bone surface is usually lined by ﬂattened,
quiescent osteoblasts (asterisks); these are
uncommon in this hyperparathyroid bone.
Osteoclasts and osteoblasts are both end-
stage cells that form by differentiation of
stromal or marrow precursors; some of the
poorly differentiated cells in the zones sur-
rounding the osteoclasts or osteoblasts are
probably precursors of the next generation
of cells. Some hematopoietic bone marrow
is also present in the section (Marrow), and
there are some blood-ﬁlled sinusoids; in
birds, the red blood cells are oval and
nucleated. B: Demonstration of bone turn-
over. Human trabecular bone photographed
at ﬂuorescence .500 nm with excitation at
400 nm to show tetracycline labeling. Tetracyclines are incorporated into bone mineral because of their affinity for hydroxyapatite and
ﬂuoresce under these conditions. Prior to biopsy, the patient received tetracycline before and after a 2-week interval. In the area
illustrated, approximately 40% of the bone surface incorporated tetracycline during either the ﬁrst or second labeling period; bone
formation in some areas was continuous; in other cases, only the ﬁrst or second label is seen. This pattern reﬂects the patchwork of
intermittent synthesis and degradation of the skeleton. The volume of bone labeled represents the amount of new bone synthesized in
the interval; clearly, over a few years, the majority of the trabecular bone would be replaced. Since formation and degradation of bone
normally balance, the extent of osteoclastic activity during the interval is similar. Unlabeled bone and marrow are faintly visible because of
resorption site,(12,13) and electron-microscopic studies of os-
teoclasts, suggesting a specialized osteoclast–bone attach-
ment structure and vacuolar transport of degraded bone
within osteoclasts.(14)Advances in cell biology and biochemis-
try have allowed much more detailed study of bone degrada-
tion in the last decade. These studies, facilitated by methods
for isolation of osteoclasts,(15,16) have provided a clear and
fairly comprehensive picture of the physiology of this cell,
including its attachment to bone, the biochemical pathways
for matrix degradation, and removal of degradative products.
The central activity of the osteoclast is acid secretion, which is
accomplished by very high expression of the vacuolar (V-type)
Figure 2. Features of the osteoclast. A: Bone-attached osteo-
clasts. This methylene blue-stained section shows an area similar
to that boxed in Figure 1A, photographed under oil immersion.
Two osteoclasts, closely apposed to bone, contain multiple nuclei
(n) in one cell. Numerous cytoplasmic organelles are present at
the limit of resolution. An additional structure at the cell–bone
interface is present but cannot be resolved. Areas including nuclei
and adjacent cytoplasm (large box) and the cell–bone interface
(small box) are similar to those shown in electron micrographs B
and C. B: Nuclei, mitochondria, and vacuoles. Transmission
electron micrograph of a portion of an avian osteoclast similar to
the large box in A, at approximately 10-fold higher resolution.
Portions of several nuclei (n) are seen. In addition, at this power it
is seen that the cell is very densely populated with mitochondria
(m). Mitochondria are very abundant in osteoclasts, reﬂecting the
energy-intensive task of dissolving bone. Osteoclastic vacuoles
(v) are also present. These are involved in both secretory and
transport functions. C: The ruffled membrane. At an image size
similar to B, the osteoclast–bone interface is resolved to show the
large increase in cell membrane area at this site, the ruffled
membrane. This contains a remarkable concentration of the
vacuolar-type H1-ATPase complex and related acid transport
proteins, which transport the HCl that dissolves bone mineral. In
addition, the cells contain vacuoles (v) of various kinds, some
here with electron-dense inclusions that may include calcium
salts and other bone products, as well as smaller vacuoles
(arrows). In occasional sections of osteoclasts, vacuoles contain-
ing recognizable collagen fragments are seen (not illustrated).
electrogenic H1-ATPase.(10) This interesting and ubiquitous
pump is a homolog of the mitochondrial or bacterial ATP
synthase (F-type ATPase), which uses a proton gradient to
make ATP rather than the converse. F-ATPases have been
studied in more detail: they operate with three sets of F1
(cytoplasmic) a and b ATP synthase/hydrolase stator sub-
units around a central (g) rotor that is connected to a proton
channel in the cell membrane; ATP synthesis or hydrolysis is
coupled to proton transport by rotation of the g subunit as a
nanomotor.(17) While the V-ATPase has not been studied as
elegantly, its subunit structure suggests that its mechanism
will be similar.(18) There are a number of subtypes and
modiﬁcations of this basic proton pump; a new isoform for the
A (catalytic stator) subunit of the V1 (cytoplasmic) subassem-
bly was cloned from human giant cell tumor, which included
two closely related A subunits, and this may represent a
specialized osteoclastic subtype.(19) The B (probable regula-
tory stator) subunits in rat and chicken osteoclasts are
previously recognized subtypes,(20,21) and the C, D, and E
subunits of the osteoclast also have been cloned but do not
appear to differ from those in other tissues.(22) The osteoclast
modiﬁes its expression of major pump subunits during its
resorption cycle,(23) and distribution of the pump is used to
control activity. In one type of osteopetrotic mouse (oc/oc),
high levels of the proton pump are present but its targeting to
the plasma membrane is lost, which is hypothesized to be
related to a lost association of pump subunits with the
Osteoclastic acid transport deposits acid onto a strongly
basic mineral, [Ca3(PO4)2]3CaOH2, so that the process moves
molar concentrations of acid to liberate calcium, rather than
millimolar concentrations to acidify membrane-bound spaces
[Ca3(PO4)2]3Ca(OH)2 1 8 H1& 10 Ca21 1 6 HPO4
There is considerable substitution of hydroxyapatite in bone
matrix,(25) but these details are beyond the scope of this
essay. In any case, several hundred milligrams of calcium are
deposited and removed from the human skeleton daily, so
large amounts of acid must be transported and osteoclastic
expression of the V-ATPase is massive. Massive activity of an
ATPase is obviously an energy-intensive process; in keeping,
osteoclasts are packed with mitochondria. The energy source
for acid secretion appears to be almost exclusively glu-
cose.(26) Because the H1-ATPase is an electrogenic pump
that transports only protons, it requires another transport
process to balance charge. This is supplied by a stilbene-
sensitive chloride channel of ,60 kDa, expressed in the
same membrane.(27,28) The source of the protons for acid
secretion is carbonic acid; its slow spontaneous generation
from CO2 produced by oxidative phosphorylation is aug-
mented by expression of carbonic anhydrase type II in
osteoclasts.(29,30) Since secretion of H1 leaves the conjugate
base within the cell, massive HCl secretion obviously also
requires a balancing base secretion to maintain osteoclast
intracellular pH within physiological limits. This is accom-
plished by a passive chloride–bicarbonate exchange in the
basolateral membrane,(31,32) providing for an overall balanced
acid secretion driven by a single active process, the H1-
These linked ion transport processes are summarized in
Figure 3. Additional characterization of the chloride channel
and chloride–bicarbonate exchanger that support acid secre-
tion may prove very interesting. Both of these steps are
potential regulators of bone degradation and deserve more
attention than they have received. Further, activation and
inactivation of the H1-ATPase, which probably involves con-
trol of membrane insertion, is an important area that has not
been adequately investigated. Additionally, osteoclast mem-
branes express calcium, potassium, and sodium channels
and pumps whose functions in overall osteoclast metabolism
are not as well characterized.(33–38) It is likely that these are
Figure 3. The acid-transport pathway of the osteoclast. The
osteoclast consumes large amounts of energy to drive HCl
secretion, which produces calcium, water, and phosphate
[Ca3(PO4)2]3CaOH2, that comprises bone mineral. The source
of the energy is oxidative phosphorylation of glucose,(26) taken
up by a GLUT2 transporter, in mitochondria. Protons are
derived from carbonic acid, a process facilitated by high
expression of carbonic anhydrase II(29) in osteoclasts. Proton
secretion is by a massively expressed V-type H1-ATPase.(10,19–24)
This electrogenic pump drives chloride cosecretion(27,28) with
cellular pH homeostasis by passive chloride–bicarbonate
exchange at the contralateral surface coupled in turn to
bicarbonate formation and chloride secretion.(31,32)
involved in cellular functions, including control mechanisms,
As it has become clear that the osteoclast secretes massive
amounts of acid into an extracellular compartment, the
mechanism of osteoclast attachment has developed as a
major research interest. Identiﬁcation of the effects of antago-
nists that block binding of arginyl-glycyl-glutamyl receptor
proteins(39) and later of the avb3 integrin receptor as a key
element(40) were important advances. The ﬁnding that anti-
body to b3 integrin alone is sufficient to interrupt osteoclastic
action in vivo(41) provides strong evidence that this particular
mechanism is required for osteoclastic attachment under
physiological conditions. Numerous observations related to
osteoclastic attachment indicate that this is a complex pro-
cess that evokes intracellular signals, and there are sugges-
tions that receptors other than the avb3 integrin may also be
involved.(42) These considerations may add depth to the
picture in a few years. Areas where clariﬁcation would be
useful include the organization of the osteoclast cytoskeleton,
which has special architecture related to the bone attachment;
(14,43) the occurrence and function of membrane-associated
proteins other than the integrins; and the physiological roles
of numerous intracellular signaling processes that may be
Enzymatic degradation of the organic matrix
Degradation of the organic matrix of bone remains a contro-
versial topic, and based on numerous studies identifying
enzymes in situ as well as isolating various metalloprotein-
ases and thiol proteinases from bone, it is probable that this
mechanism involves several enzymes. However, a major
advance resulted recently from analysis of mammalian osteoclast
cDNA libraries, the discovery of a new thiol proteinase,(44–46)
cathepsin K, which is expressed prominently in mammalian
osteoclasts.(47) Indeed, this line of research led rapidly to the
identiﬁcation of cathepsin K expression deﬁcit as the molecu-
lar cause of pycnodysostosis (Toulouse-Lautrec disease).(48)
Cathepsin K is the predominant proteinase in human osteo-
clasts.(49) The defects in pycnodysostosis, short stature and
osteosclerosis, constitute a form of osteopetrosis compatible
with life, however, pointing to a leading, but not exclusive, role
for cathepsin K in bone collagen turnover. Acid proteinases,
including the aspartate proteinase cathepsin D and the thiol
proteinases cathepsins B and L that are also present in
osteoclasts,(50–52) would presumably be important in pycnodys-
ostosis, while playing subsidiary roles in normal matrix
degradation. Further, metalloproteinases that have been
identiﬁed in bone(53–56) may well be important in dissolution of
nonmineralized matrix to enable the osteoclast to form its
tight seal for extracellular acidiﬁcation. The processing and
trafficking of proteinases in bone has not been extensively
investigated, although available evidence points to a mannose-
6-phosphate receptor-dependent transport mechanism.(57,58)
Questions regarding how the products of acidic bone degra-
dation are trafficked by the osteoclast have been widely
debated. Opinion has been divided between a detachment
and reattachment process to allow degraded products to be
released, movement of calcium through the cytoplasm by ion
pumps, and transcytosis via a vesicular process. New tech-
niques allowing direct observation of vesicular transcytosis of
labeled bone products(59,60) now show clearly that the vesicu-
lar transcytosis process is important in bone-resorbing osteo-
clasts. There may still be roles for bone-detachment and
ion-transport processes, but there can be little doubt that
vesicular transcytosis is a key mechanism. Teleologically, this
makes excellent sense. The cell expends tremendous energy
to produce its low pH extracellular degradative environment,
which with dissolution of bone mineral develops a very high
calcium activity.(61) For calcium to transit the cell other than in
membrane-delimited form, a great deal of additional energy
would be wasted by allowing it to run down a very steep
gradient, then to be pumped at considerable expense out of
the cell. Similarly, if it were necessary for the osteoclast to
detach to eliminate resorption products, the process of
reattaching and re-creating the acid gradient would expend
unnecessarily substantial energy. Remaining challenges in
this specialty area include description of the membrane and
cytoskeletal elements mediating the complex vesicular traffic
Key elements in osteoclastic degradation are summarized
in Figure 4. The giant cell seals an area of bone, a process
involving the integrin avb3.(39–41) Into this area, HCl is secreted
to dissolve the bone mineral (detailed in Fig. 3). Collagen is
degraded by a specialized thiol proteinase active under the
same acidic conditions, cathepsin K, produced in large
quantities by the osteoclast.(44–49) Degradation products are
removed during resorption by vesicular transcytosis.(14,59,60)
These components are conserved in the terrestrial verte-
brates, but some details may vary in distantly related species.
Control of osteoclastic activity involves
Diverse forces driving bone removal and deposition are
balanced by complex control mechanisms.(62) In adult hu-
mans, calcium homeostasis and repair of wear are usually
the major factors that inﬂuence bone turnover, although
long-term skeletal loss is clearly also linked to processes
including use of the skeleton to balance chronic acid loads.(63)
If bone structure is lost (osteoporosis), structural failure
ultimately occurs. The strength of the bone is also compro-
mised when bone turnover is interrupted for a prolonged
period, even if overall bone mass remains adequate.
It is the control processes that manage osteoclast differen-
tiation and activity which currently pose the most challenging
problems in osteoclast biology. The basic physical mecha-
nisms of osteoclast function are fairly comprehensively de-
scribed, although functionally important processes still await
detailed characterization. However, the mechanisms regulat-
ing bone turnover are much less well established. Since the
osteoclast participates in bone development and growth,
normal turnover and response to changes in loading, electro-
lyte homeostasis, and repair processes, it appears highly
likely that multiple pathways control osteoclast differentiation
and regulation of mature cells. In this ﬁnal section, we will
consider selected ﬁndings regarding processes that control
osteoclast differentiation and modulation of activity of mature
Control of osteoclast differentiation
Precursors of the monocyte–macrophage family are required
to produce osteoclasts,(9) and under most circumstances,
efficient differentiation requires contact with supporting stro-
mal cells.(64) On the other hand, tartrate-resistant acid phos-
phatase-expressing cells have been produced directly from
macrophages with a cytokine cocktail including interleukin-1
(IL-1), IL-3, and colony-stimulating factor-1 (CSF-1).(65) The
meaning of this result vis-a`-vis normal bone turnover is
unclear; in the usual case, where both osteoblast-like cells
and macrophages are present, formation of osteoclasts does
seem to be directly related to, and requires, cell–cell con-
tact.(64) However, these apparently disparate ﬁndings may
simply point to multiple pathways for osteoclast formation.
For the most part, bone turnover replaces living bone in an
orderly fashion, and input from osteoblasts would not be
surprising. However, in the case of fractured or necrotic bone,
where there are no living osteoblasts or stromal cells, osteo-
clasts do form and degrade the dead bone. Thus, it would be
expected that such alternate mechanisms for osteoclast
formation exist and would function under circumstances
where osteoblasts and other matrix cells are absent.
Monocyte–macrophage precursor cells are required to
form osteoclasts, so those factors important in macrophage
development, including CSF-1 (c-fms ligand), are also re-
quired for osteoclast differentiation.(66) However, these gener-
ally relate to steps of differentiation that are not major control
points for bone catabolism since they are also required to
produce macrophages. Other signals required for osteoclast
differentiation include the steroid 1,25-dihydroxyvitamin D.
This systemic hormone has major effects on cartilage, osteo-
blasts, osteoclasts, and organs. Parathyroid hormone has a
dramatic upregulatory effect on osteoblast activity and osteo-
clast formation, although osteoclasts can form in its absence.
How vitamin D and parathyroid hormone signals inﬂuence
osteoclast development and control is complex, and addi-
tional mechanistic study is needed. It is possible that some of
these effects are mediated through osteoblasts and chondro-
cytes rather than direct effects on the osteoclast, although, as
discussed above, osteoclasts also form without contacting
other bone cells. From the standpoint of basic mechanisms,
the recent ﬁnding that antibodies to CD98 promote efficient
osteoclast formation directly(67) is particularly exciting since it
suggests a simple mechanism that may be a common factor
in osteoclast differentiation pathways.
The hypothesis that specialized expression of local growth
factors is important in terminal osteoclast differentiation is a
subject of intense investigation. Candidate signals include
hepatocyte growth factor (c-met ligand) and stem-cell factor
(c-kit ligand).(68–70) Other factors that affect osteoclast differen-
tiation and activity include prostaglandin E and interleukins
such as IL-1 and IL-6.(66) These latter factors have been
extensively investigated and probably do not, of themselves,
allow the key step from preosteoclast to osteoclast to pro-
ceed. It is possible that multiple factors may be required in the
ﬁnal stages of osteoclast formation, however, and that presen-
tation of multiple factors simultaneously will be important.
Within the osteoclast, differentiation involves elements includ-
ing c-fos and PU.1.(71,72) Differentiation-associated transcrip-
tion factors are promising tools for determining the pathways
of osteoclast differentiation more completely. However, it may
Figure 4. Cellular specialization of the osteoclast. A multi-
nucleated acid-secreting osteoclast is schematically repre-
sented as having three nuclei (shaded ovals) and a convo-
luted acid-secreting surface with vacuolar-type H1-ATPases.
Osteoclast formation under some conditions requires contact
with stromal cells,(64) but there are exceptions.(65,67) The
integrin avb3(39–41) is required for formation of the extracellular
acid compartment. Synthesis and secretion of a novel thiol
proteinase, cathepsin K,(44–49) degrades bone collagen in acid.
Removal of degraded collagen and ionized calcium is by
vesicular transcytosis.(14,59,60)
be difficult to separate control of differentiation from modula-
tion of activity in mature osteoclasts.
Another aspect of the differentiation puzzle is how effete
osteoclasts are removed. The available evidence points to an
apoptotic mechanism, although this aspect of the osteoclast
life cycle is not well studied. Factors that induce apoptosis
may include nitric oxide (NO).(73) Apoptosis may well be
important in such aspects as steroid-dependent reduction in
overall bone degradation caused by glucocorticoids and
estrogens,(74,75) and this area holds considerable promise for
development of new antiosteoporotic therapeutic agents. As
with other aspects of osteoclast biology, it is important to
interpret the isolated ﬁndings cautiously because many regu-
latory pathways coexist. For example, excellent work has
demonstrated that rat osteoclast apoptosis is enhanced by
glucocorticoids,(74) but glucocorticoid treatment in humans
Cytokines and intracellular control pathways
that modulate osteoclast activity
Other control processes undoubtedly function in mature
osteoclasts. Systems that have prominent effects on osteo-
clast activity include tyrosine kinases, and the osteoclast has
a very strong dependence on c-src(76) with downstream
signals including c-Cbl.(77) Intercellular signals in bone in-
clude NO, with effects on intracellular targets including
cGMP-dependent protein kinase.(78–80) The small peptide
calcitonin, which is an important downregulator of the osteo-
clast in ﬁsh, has variable effects in higher vertebrates, where
the peptide is also a neurological signal; the effects of this
interesting peptide hormone and other small peptide hor-
mones on osteoclasts have been reviewed elsewhere.(81)
Additional intracellular control processes include calcium-
responsive proteins(82) and a variety of kinases, including
members of the A, C, and phosphatidylinositol-dependent
protein kinase families.(83–85) Other possible pathways include
ion (voltage) gradients besides calcium, considered in the
preceding section. Steroids other than vitamin D, particularly
estrogens, affect osteoclast formation,(86) but whether these
also modify activity of mature osteoclasts is controversial.(87)
Understanding this complex web of control processes will
require considerable additional investigation. Major gaps in
understanding exist regarding whether or how effects discov-
ered by blocking or activating any of several dozen pathways
reﬂect physiological cellular mechanisms of osteoclasts. Com-
plex cellular control processes are expected since the skel-
eton responds to mechanical load, calcium, pH, and other
factors. Stepwise understanding of complete pathways of
physiological control and the interrelations of many such
pathways will be challenging work.
Conclusion: future directions and controversies
There are important unresolved questions in several aspects
of osteoclast biology. These include whether additional bio-
chemical pathways are important in bone degradation, what
control mechanisms are involved in normal osteoclast differ-
entiation, the functional signiﬁcance of several osteoclast
intracellular regulatory pathways, and which extracellular
signals regulate bone degradation in mature osteoclasts
under physiological conditions.
While some central biochemical pathways that mediate
the acid degradation of bone are established (Figs. 3 and 4),
there are a number of alternative and additional pathways
that may be important. These include the role of proteinases
other than cathepsin K and the detailed biochemical path-
ways responsible for cell fusion, vesicular transport, and
formation of the ruffled membrane.
The mechanisms of osteoclast differentiation include poorly
described cytokine-regulated pathways that may allow or
promote cell differentiation. Some promoter elements related
to osteoclast differentiation have been discovered, and the
number of cytokines under investigation is manageable.
Thus, many of the uncertainties should be resolved in a few
years. It is expected that some controversies may be re-
solved by demonstrating how different stimuli may indepen-
dently regulate common pathways in cell differentiation,
depending on the reason that osteoclast formation is re-
quired. Stimuli for osteoclast formation vary widely, from local
removal of mineralized cartilage in fetal bone to low plasma
calcium activity with consequent parathyroid hormone signal-
Intracellular regulatory pathways are an area of very active
interest. The osteoclast contains control pathways similar to
other macrophage family cells, and activators and inhibitors
of pathways, including calcium and cyclic nucleotides, cause
measurable differences in cellular activity. However, sorting
the physiological signiﬁcance of these effects and what
modulates the signaling will be a complex task. In addition,
such work may establish the functional role of ion transport-
ers, such as rectifying potassium channels, that are present
in osteoclasts but have no clear purpose.
Extracellular regulatory pathways that modify the activity
of osteoclasts after differentiation are complex and remain
undetermined. As with factors that control cell differentiation,
there may be multiple valid results. The osteoclast functions
in skeletal differentiation, skeletal turnover, calcium and
acid-base homeostasis, and removal of necrotic bone. De-
pending on the circumstances, it is likely that the factors
controlling activity of the osteoclast will differ. When different
results are obtained in varying conditions, these may provide
opportunities to determine interactions of multiple pathways.
The conservative maintenance of bone density during
growth and over many cycles of turnover has led to a popular
hypothesis that bone formation and degradation are linked at
the level of cell differentiation. Bone-forming and bone-
degrading cells do appear to have cross-regulatory mecha-
nisms. However, it now appears unlikely that there will be a
single ‘‘linking factor’’ that maintains bone mass. Regulation
of bone mass is complex, and many types of regulatory
activity probably have physiological roles under particular
